card.title: The Viruses That Hunt Bacteria
card.teaser: They look like lunar landers, outnumber every other lifeform, and might just replace antibiotics.
card.coverImagePrompt: A bacteriophage with geometric icosahedral head and spindly leg fibers landing on the surface of a bacterium, electron-microscope aesthetic with dramatic teal and violet lighting, cinematic close-up

lessons.title: The most abundant thing alive
lessons.body: There are more bacteriophages on Earth than every other organism combined — stars in the galaxy don't even come close. A single milliliter of seawater holds around 10 million of them. They drift everywhere, and every one is hunting a bacterium.

visual.imagePrompt: A drop of seawater magnified to show millions of tiny phage particles suspended like constellations, deep-blue cinematic microscopy

lessons.title: Tiny spacecraft with a mission
lessons.body: A bacteriophage, or 'phage,' is a virus that infects only bacteria. Many species look uncannily like a lunar lander: a geometric head packed with DNA, a stalk-like tail, and spidery fibers that clamp onto the target. They can't move on their own — they bump into prey at random and only stick to the exact bacterial species they evolved to match.

keyTerms.definition: A virus that infects bacteria; 'phage' comes from Greek for 'eater'
The protein shell that encloses a virus's genetic material

visual.imagePrompt: A labeled diagram of a T4 bacteriophage showing capsid, tail sheath, baseplate, and tail fibers, technical illustration

lessons.title: The hijack
lessons.body: When a phage latches on, it punctures the bacterial wall and injects its DNA like a syringe. That DNA hijacks the cell's machinery, turning the bacterium into a factory for new phages — often hundreds in under half an hour. When the cell is full, new phages release enzymes that burst it open, and the cycle starts again.

visual.imagePrompt: A phage perched on a bacterium, needle-like tail piercing the cell wall, glowing DNA injecting inside, dramatic sci-fi illustration

lessons.title: A comeback in medicine
lessons.body: Phage therapy was discovered in 1917, then largely abandoned in the West once antibiotics arrived. The Soviet Union kept using it. Now, with antibiotic resistance rising, phages are back: in 2019, doctors used genetically engineered phages to save a teenager dying of a Mycobacterium infection after a lung transplant. It was the first documented success with engineered phages in a human.
lessons.funFact: Because each phage only kills specific bacteria, a phage cocktail can wipe out an infection without flattening your gut microbiome the way broad-spectrum antibiotics do.

visual.imagePrompt: A hospital vial labeled 'phage therapy' beside a petri dish showing clear plaques where phages have killed bacteria

lessons.title: Predators at the smallest scale
lessons.body: Phages have been shaping bacterial evolution for billions of years — the biggest predator-prey dance in nature, fought at the nanometer scale. They're not just a curiosity; they're the reason oceans don't drown in bacteria, and they may be a major tool in medicine's next chapter.

quiz.question: Roughly how many phages live in a typical milliliter of seawater?
quiz.explanation: Surveys typically find around 10 million phage particles per milliliter — making them the most abundant biological entity on Earth.

quiz.question: How does a phage get its genetic material into a bacterium?
options: It is swallowed whole by the bacterium
It injects DNA through the cell wall like a syringe
It fuses its membrane with the bacterium's
quiz.explanation: A phage clamps onto the bacterium with its tail fibers, then punches through the wall and injects its DNA.

quiz.question: What advantage do phages have over broad-spectrum antibiotics?
options: They are highly specific and spare other bacteria
quiz.explanation: Each phage typically targets a narrow set of bacteria, leaving the rest of the microbiome intact.

sources.title: Marine viruses — major players in the global ecosystem
Engineered bacteriophages for treatment of a patient with a disseminated Mycobacterium abscessus infection
